data.title: Home Depot Shares Gain As Oil Crash Lifts Consumer Stocks
data.description:
• Home Depot stock is showing exceptional strength. What’s driving HD stock higher?

Oil Rout Fuels Broader Risk-On Relief Trade

The oil collapse appears to be tied to traders rapidly unwinding positions that had priced in a prolonged Strait of Hormuz disruption, with S&P 500 futures rising nearly 3% in response. Markets also moved to price in a friendlier macro backdrop, including lower inflation pressure and a greater chance of future rate cuts if energy prices remain contained.

That matters for Home Depot because it is not just a generic retail stock. Home Depot is the largest home-improvement retailer in the U.S., with business tied closely to big-ticket renovation demand, contractor activity, seasonal outdoor projects and overall housing-related consumer spending.

Lower Energy Costs Can Support Home Project Demand

When oil drops sharply, it can ease pressure on fuel, freight and other input costs across the economy while also leaving consumers with a bit more breathing room.

For HD, that combination can support sentiment in two ways: it improves the outlook for consumer discretionary spending on home projects, and it may strengthen the broader housing backdrop if cooler energy prices help reinforce expectations for easier monetary policy.

HD Momentum Stabilizes After Pullback

Home Depot's RSI has mostly stayed in the neutral range (30–70) over the past six months, with a brief overbought stretch near 70 followed by a steady cooldown.

More recently, the RSI dipped toward oversold levels near 30 before rebounding into the mid-40s, suggesting momentum is stabilizing but not yet strongly bullish.

Analyst Consensus On Home Depot Remains Bullish

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $423.00. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Telsey Advisory Group: Outperform (Maintains Target to $435.00) (March 25)
  • BNP Paribas: Neutral (Lowers Target to $348.00) (March 24)
  • UBS: Buy (Raises Target to $450.00) (Feb. 25)

HD Shares Climb 5% Wednesday

HD Price Action: Home Depot shares were up 5.26% at $335.54 at the time of publication on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: EU, Australia Seal Free Trade Deal After 8 Years Of Talks, Covering €89B
data.description:
After eight years of negotiations, the European Union (EU) and Australia have concluded a landmark free trade agreement – one driven partly by concerns about US and Chinese trade policies.

The agreement eliminates over 99% of tariffs on EU exports to Australia. The deal scraps duties on nearly all Australian goods and critical minerals entering the EU. It opens the EU's high-income market of 450 million consumers.

Source: Eurostat

Formal negotiations began in June 2018 but stalled in October 2023. At that point, Australian Trade Minister Don Farrell demanded new beef and sheep meat export quotas. The EU negotiators described it as incompatible with their final offer.

They revived the talks again in 2025 in response to the Trump administration's imposition of tariffs across the globe. Von der Leyen and Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese signed the deal in Canberra on March 24.

EU Runs Trade Surplus with Australia

The EU runs a goods trade surplus of €28 billion with Australia, exporting €37 billion in goods in 2025. The bloc imports just €10.7 billion in goods.

In services, the imbalance is equally pronounced. EU firms supplied €31 billion to Australian clients in 2024, against €11 billion flowing the other way.

EU exports to Australia are expected to grow by as much as 33% over the next decade. They could reach up to €17.7 billion annually, according to the Latvian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Tariff reductions will save EU exporters approximately €1 billion annually, it said.

EU Investment in Australia Could Surge

Key sectors with strong growth potential for the EU include dairy, expected to increase by up to 48%. The bloc’s motor vehicles exports could increase by 52% and chemicals by 20%.

EU investment in Australia has the potential to grow by over 87%. Australian tariffs on European wine, sparkling wine, chocolates, and fresh produce will fall to zero immediately.

Australia will also protect 165 EU geographical indications (GI) to create formal barriers against imitation products. Items range from Comté cheese to Irish whiskey.

Europe Repositions on Critical Minerals

Critical minerals sit at the strategic heart of the agreement. China currently controls roughly 90% of global rare earth processing of minerals.

Beijing has tightened export controls on key resources over the past two years. This has left Europe scrambling to secure alternative supply chains as its green and digital transitions accelerate. The deal also aligns with the EU's broader push under the Critical Raw Materials Act to reduce strategic dependencies and secure long‑term industrial resilience.

"What has become clear in the past two years is that we should never be too dependent on other partners when it comes to critical raw materials," Holger Görg at the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, a Germany-based economic research institute, said.

Under the agreement, all tariffs on Australian energy and resources exports to the EU will be permanently eliminated, including those applied to lithium hydroxide, hydrogen, and associated carriers, which had faced duties of up to around 5.5%.

"Removing all tariffs on resources and critical minerals strengthens Australia's competitiveness and supports predictable, open trade with a key strategic partner," Tania Constable, CEO of the Minerals Council of Australia, said.

European Automakers & Pharmaceuticals Set to Gain

European automakers stand to gain the most visible near-term upside from eliminated vehicle tariffs and the reformed EV tax threshold.

Alongside the FTA, both parties signed a Security and Defense Partnership and launched formal negotiations for Australia's association with Horizon Europe, the world's largest research funding program.

Agriculture Was Most Contentious Issue

The agreement's most contentious aspect is agriculture, where both sides are simultaneously losing the argument domestically. For Australian beef and lamb exporters, the final quotas fell far short of industry expectations.

"What the Australian Government has accepted today appears to offer no material change for key agricultural commodities as what the Government rightly rejected in October 2023," Australia's National Farmers Federation said.

European farmers are equally discontented, but for opposite reasons.

Copa-Cogeca, the EU's influential pan-European farmers' lobby, called the concessions "unacceptable," warning that "European farmers cannot continue to absorb the cost of bilateral trade liberalization without adequate and truly effective safeguards."

Free Trade Reflects Open Trade Among Democracies

The EU–Australia FTA reflects a shared conviction that supply chain resilience and strategic autonomy now rank alongside growth as core trade objectives.

The agreement must still clear the EU Council, European Parliament, and Australian parliamentary processes before entering into force. This process could take at least one to two years, and it isn't guaranteed.

The EU–Mercosur trade agreement is still undergoing judicial review. The European Parliament referred the deal to the European Court of Justice to assess whether it complies with the EU Treaties.

If approved, the Australia deal would mark one of the EU's most strategically aligned trade partnerships in the Indo‑Pacific, reinforcing a shift toward values‑based economic alliances.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga's reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Axon Stock Is Rebounding Today: What's Happening?
data.description:
• Axon Enterprise stock is among today’s top performers. Why are AXON shares rallying?

Axon's rebound follows a sharp risk-off tape on Tuesday, when the Nasdaq fell 1.16% and the S&P 500 shed 0.73% after durable goods orders declined 1.4% in February. Traders are watching whether Wednesday's strength can stick after risk-off volatility across the market pressured high-multiple names.

Notable Developments

Goldman Sachs analyst Michael Ng maintained a Buy rating on Axon and lowered its price target to $625 from $720.

Additionally, the broader market is surging today, with the Nasdaq (QQQ) up 2.93% and the S&P 500 (SPY) up 2.37%. Industrials (XLI) are leading sector gains at 3.73%, and Axon's rally is happening in a session where market breadth is strongly positive.

Axon Technical Analysis

At $398.15, Axon is trading 14.8% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA), the stock's average price over the last 20 sessions, which suggests the short-term trend is still pointed down. It's also 24.8% below its 100-day SMA, indicating the intermediate trend remains under pressure even after today's bounce.

The relative strength index (RSI), a momentum gauge, is 24.16, which is consistent with oversold conditions after heavy selling pressure. That oversold reading can coincide with sharp rebounds, but it also reflects how stretched the recent downtrend has become.

  • Key Support: $396.50 — a near-term line where buyers have recently shown up.

Axon’s Background

Axon is building a public safety operating system by integrating hardware devices with cloud software. Its lineup spans cloud-hosted digital evidence management, TASER energy devices, body and in-car cameras, drones and robotic security, plus training tools.

The business is split into Software and Services and Connected Devices, with most revenue coming from Connected Devices (TASER, cameras, drones and accessories/warranties). That mix matters for the stock's reaction to analyst notes because investors often weigh hardware demand and subscription-like software expansion differently when thinking about longer-term growth and valuation.

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $725.71. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Goldman Sachs: Buy (Lowers Target to $625.00) (April 8)
  • UBS: Neutral (Lowers Target to $570.00) (Feb. 25)
  • Barclays: Overweight (Lowers Target to $682.00) (Feb. 25)

Benzinga Edge Rankings: The Benzinga Edge scorecard for Axon highlights its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market.

  • Momentum: Bearish (Score: 3.37) — The stock's trend signals remain very weak versus the broader tape.
  • Value: Bearish (Score: 1.07) — The market is pricing in a lot of optimism relative to fundamentals.
  • Growth: Neutral (Score: 64.1) — Growth factors screen as solid, but not strong enough to offset weak momentum.

The Verdict: Axon’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a growth-leaning story that's currently being weighed down by very weak momentum and a stretched valuation profile. For longer-term investors, the setup reads like "good business, tough chart," where improving trend signals would matter as much as growth execution.

AXON Price Action: Axon shares were up 5.99% at $395.19 at the time of publication on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock
